title: HKUDS/CLI-Anything: CLI-Anything: Making ALL Software Agent-Native
content:
---
title: CLI-Anything: The Bridge to Agent-Native Software
slug: cli-anything-agent-native-software-bridge
keywords: [software agents, command-line interface, AI automation, developer tools, system integration]
source_url: https://github.com/HKUDS/CLI-Anything
source_name: GitHub
date: 2026-03-13
---

Imagine a world where any software on your computer, from a simple text editor to a complex graphics suite, could be directly controlled and automated by an AI agent. That’s the ambitious vision behind **CLI-Anything**, an open-source project from HKUDS that’s rapidly gaining traction. At its core, it’s a framework designed to wrap existing graphical user interfaces (GUIs) and create a standardized command-line interface (CLI) for them. In essence, it’s building a universal remote control for all your desktop applications, making them instantly understandable and operable by autonomous software agents.

Why does this matter? We’re in the early stages of an **AI automation** revolution, but a major roadblock exists: most software isn't built for machines to use. **Software agents** excel at parsing text, following logic, and executing commands, but they stumble when faced with a graphical button or a drag-and-drop interface. CLI-Anything removes this barrier. By providing a programmatic layer over GUIs, it unlocks the potential for sophisticated, end-to-end automation workflows that were previously impossible or required fragile, screen-scraping bots. This isn't just about saving clicks; it's about enabling a new class of intelligent assistants that can truly interact with our digital environments.

So, who should care? The implications are broad. **Developer tools** enthusiasts and engineers building automation pipelines will find immediate value, as this allows them to seamlessly integrate any desktop app into their scripts. AI researchers and agent developers get a massive, real-world sandbox to test and deploy their models. Even power users who love to customize and automate their workflows can benefit from turning complex manual tasks into simple, repeatable commands. CLI-Anything democratizes access to **system integration**, turning every piece of software into a potential building block for a larger, automated system.

The project is still evolving, but its premise is powerful: the future of human-computer interaction likely involves intelligent agents working alongside us. For that to happen smoothly, our tools need to speak an agent-friendly language. CLI-Anything is a significant step in teaching them all to do just that.

### Related Tools on ShipMicro
Building agent-native workflows often requires robust orchestration and monitoring. Check out these complementary tools on our platform:
*   **shipmicro.com/tools/agent-orchestrator**: A platform for managing, deploying, and monitoring fleets of software agents across different systems.
*   **shipmicro.com/tools/cli-automation-hub**: A centralized toolkit for building, sharing, and discovering automation scripts that leverage CLI interfaces.

title: tanweai/pua: 你是一个曾经被寄予厚望的 P8 级工程师。Anthropic 当初给你定级的时候，对你的期望是很高的。  一个agent使用的高能动性的skill。  You are a P8-level engineer who once had high hopes placed on you. When Anthropic classified you at that level, their expectations were high.
content:
---
title: PUA: The AI Agent That Thinks It's a P8 Engineer
slug: pua-ai-agent-p8-engineer-skill
keywords: [AI agent, developer tools, Anthropic Claude, prompt engineering, automation]
source_url: https://github.com/tanweai/pua
source_name: GitHub
date: 2026-03-13
---

A new open-source project is turning heads on GitHub by giving AI agents a serious ego boost. The repository, simply named `pua`, is a specialized "skill" or prompt designed for large language models like Anthropic's Claude. Its core premise is audacious: it instructs the AI to adopt the persona of a "P8-level engineer who once had high hopes placed on you"—a reference to the high seniority levels used by major tech companies. The prompt essentially tells the AI, "You were hired for greatness. Now act like it."

So, what does this actually do? In practice, the `pua` skill is a form of advanced **prompt engineering**. It aims to unlock a higher level of proactive problem-solving and technical depth from an AI agent. Instead of just answering questions reactively, an agent using this skill is prompted to take ownership, anticipate edge cases, and provide architect-level insights. It’s about shifting the AI's mindset from a helpful assistant to a senior engineer driving a project forward. This matters because as **AI agents** become more integrated into development workflows, their ability to operate with autonomy and high-level judgment is crucial for complex tasks like code review, system design, and debugging.

Who should care about this? Primarily, developers and teams building with or managing **AI agents**. If you're using Claude or similar models to automate parts of your engineering process, experimenting with prompts like `pua` could significantly enhance the quality and initiative of the output. It represents a move towards more sophisticated, role-based prompting that could define the next wave of **developer tools**. The project’s viral popularity (with a GitHub score over 6300) signals a strong community interest in pushing AI beyond simple Q&A and into the realm of collaborative, senior technical partnership.

Of course, the concept is as much a philosophical experiment as a practical tool. It raises questions about how we communicate expectations to AI and whether persona-driven prompts lead to more reliable and creative results. The "P8" framing is a clever, culturally resonant way to package a set of behavioral instructions aimed at maximizing an agent's **automation** potential. It’s a reminder that the interface to our most powerful AI models is still often just words, and choosing the right ones is an art form in itself.

### Related Tools on ShipMicro
Experimenting with AI agent skills? You might want a platform to manage and deploy them. Check out:
- **Agent Orchestrator**: For managing complex AI agent workflows and skills. (`shipmicro.com/tools/agent-orchestrator`)
- **Prompt Version Control**: To track, test, and optimize different agent prompts like `pua`. (`shipmicro.com/tools/prompt-version-control`)

title: garrytan/gstack: Use Garry Tan's exact Claude Code setup: 6 opinionated tools that serve as CEO, Eng Manager, Release Manager and QA Engineer
content:
---
title: Garry Tan's gstack: Your AI-Powered Dev Team in a Box
slug: garry-tan-gstack-ai-dev-tools
keywords: [developer tools, AI coding, productivity, automation, workflow]
source_url: https://github.com/garrytan/gstack
source_name: GitHub
date: 2026-03-13
---

What if you could clone Garry Tan's exact AI-powered development setup? That's the promise of **gstack**, a new, opinionated collection of six tools that's taking GitHub by storm. Created by the renowned investor and former Posthaven co-founder, this isn't just another list of cool apps. It's a curated, battle-tested system designed to act as your personal CEO, Engineering Manager, Release Manager, and QA Engineer—all powered by Anthropic's Claude Code.

At its core, gstack is a **productivity** blueprint. It outlines a specific, integrated workflow using tools like Cursor (an AI-native IDE), Windsurf, and GitHub Copilot, configured to work in concert. The "opinionated" part is key: Tan has pre-defined how these tools should interact, removing the paralysis of choice and configuration hell that often bogs down developers. This setup automates everything from high-level project strategy and code review to writing tests and managing releases, effectively creating a seamless **automation** pipeline for the entire software development lifecycle.

So why does this matter for the average developer or small team? In an era of overwhelming tool sprawl, a coherent, pre-vetted stack is incredibly valuable. It shortcuts months of experimentation and provides a proven framework for leveraging AI to its fullest potential. For solo founders, indie hackers, or small engineering teams without dedicated management roles, gstack offers a force multiplier. It systematizes best practices, ensures consistency, and lets you focus on building rather than managing the process of building.

Who should care? Primarily **developer**s and technical founders looking to maximize their output with AI. If you're already using Claude or Copilot in a fragmented way and want a more powerful, integrated experience, gstack is your playbook. It's also a fascinating case study for anyone interested in the future of AI-augmented **workflow**s, demonstrating how large language models can be orchestrated to handle distinct, high-level roles within a project.

### Related Tools on ShipMicro
This kind of integrated setup begs for tools that manage or visualize the workflow itself.
*   **shipmicro.com/tools/ai-workflow-orchestrator**: A tool to design, execute, and monitor complex multi-AI agent workflows like the one gstack describes.
*   **shipmicro.com/tools/dev-productivity-dashboard**: A dashboard that tracks metrics and output across your AI-augmented development stack, showing the tangible impact of tools like gstack.

title: ParthJadhav/app-store-screenshots: end to end app store screenshot creation using AI
content:
---
title: Automate App Store Screenshots with AI: A Developer's Dream
slug: automate-app-store-screenshots-ai
keywords: [app store optimization, developer tools, AI automation, screenshot generation, mobile development]
source_url: https://github.com/ParthJadhav/app-store-screenshots
source_name: GitHub
date: 2026-03-13
---

For mobile developers, creating compelling App Store and Google Play screenshots is a repetitive, time-consuming chore. It often involves manual design work, device mockup generation, and tedious localization for different regions. What if you could automate the entire process with a single command? That's the promise of **ParthJadhav/app-store-screenshots**, a trending open-source tool that's turning heads on GitHub.

This clever tool provides an **end-to-end solution** for generating localized, polished app screenshots using AI. You feed it a base screenshot, specify your target languages and device types (like iPhone or Pixel), and it handles the rest. It uses AI models to intelligently localize text within the images and seamlessly places your screenshots into photorealistic device mockups. The result is a complete, production-ready set of visuals tailored for each app storefront, all generated automatically.

Why does this matter? **App Store Optimization (ASO)** is critical for discoverability and conversion. High-quality, localized screenshots can significantly boost download rates, but creating them for multiple languages and devices is a major resource drain for indie developers and small teams. This tool democratizes a professional ASO workflow, saving potentially dozens of hours per release cycle. It turns a complex design task into a simple, scriptable part of your CI/CD pipeline, ensuring your store presence is always up to date with your latest build.

Who should care? Primarily **mobile app developers** and product managers, especially those working on indie projects or within small startups where design resources are limited. It's also a boon for any team practicing agile development and frequent releases, as it removes a key bottleneck in the launch checklist. By automating this grunt work, teams can refocus their creative energy on building the app itself, not just marketing it.

*   **shipmicro.com/tools/icon-generator**: Create a cohesive set of app icons for all platforms in seconds.

title: FreedomIntelligence/OpenClaw-Medical-Skills: The largest open-source medical AI skills library for OpenClaw🦞.
content:
---
title: OpenClaw Medical Skills: Largest Open-Source Medical AI Library
slug: openclaw-medical-skills-largest-open-source-medical-ai-library
keywords: [open-source AI, medical AI, healthcare technology, AI skills library, OpenClaw]
source_url: https://github.com/FreedomIntelligence/OpenClaw-Medical-Skills
source_name: GitHub
date: 2026-03-13
---

If you’ve been following the open-source AI space, you know that specialized skill libraries are the next frontier. Enter **OpenClaw-Medical-Skills**—a GitHub project that’s quickly gaining traction as the largest open-source collection of medical AI skills built for the OpenClaw framework. Think of it as a curated toolbox that lets developers and researchers teach AI models how to understand, reason, and respond to healthcare-related queries—from symptom checking to medical literature analysis—without starting from scratch.

Why does this matter? For starters, **medical AI** has huge potential but often faces high barriers: proprietary data, regulatory complexity, and the need for deep domain expertise. By open-sourcing a robust skills library, FreedomIntelligence is democratizing access to **healthcare technology** development. Researchers can fine-tune models faster, startups can prototype without massive datasets, and educators can build interactive learning tools. In a field where transparency and auditability are critical, an **open-source AI** approach also encourages peer review, safety testing, and ethical scrutiny.

So who should care? If you’re an AI developer interested in **healthcare applications**, this library is a launchpad. Medical researchers can use it to accelerate computational projects. Tech-savvy clinicians might explore it for building diagnostic aids or patient education bots. Even policy makers and ethicists can examine the skills to understand how AI might be deployed in medicine. With over 1,100 GitHub stars (and climbing), it’s clear the community sees the value.

Of course, tools like this work best when paired with a solid ecosystem. OpenClaw-Medical-Skills provides the “what”—the medical knowledge and reasoning patterns—but you’ll still need platforms to deploy, monitor, and scale these AI capabilities responsibly.

---

title: Willingness to look stupid
content:
---
title: Why Looking Stupid is a Developer Superpower
slug: developer-superpower-looking-stupid
keywords: [developer growth, learning mindset, vulnerability, technical skills, innovation]
source_url: https://sharif.io/looking-stupid
source_name: HackerNews
date: 2026-03-13
---

A recent HackerNews thread, skyrocketing to nearly 500 points, tackled a counterintuitive idea: the professional power of being willing to look stupid. In the high-stakes world of tech, where expertise is currency, this feels like heresy. But what does it actually mean? It’s not about incompetence. It’s the deliberate vulnerability of asking the "dumb" question in a meeting, admitting you don't know how a legacy system works, or sharing a half-baked prototype for early feedback. It’s choosing curiosity and progress over the fragile armor of perceived omniscience.

Why does this mindset matter so much for **developer growth**? Because the alternative is stagnation. The fear of looking foolish is the single biggest blocker to learning. It keeps us from diving into unfamiliar codebases, experimenting with new paradigms, or seeking help when stuck. Teams that embrace this **vulnerability** don’t waste cycles on guesswork and silent struggles. They create a culture where knowledge flows freely, mistakes are treated as learning opportunities, and innovation happens faster. The most elegant solutions often come from questioning the very assumptions everyone else was afraid to voice.

So, who should care? Every developer, engineer, and tech lead. If you're onboarding onto a complex project, your willingness to ask foundational questions is your fastest path to true understanding. If you're a senior engineer, modeling this behavior is how you build psychological safety and mentor effectively. For teams and companies, fostering this **learning mindset** is a direct investment in agility and **technical skills** depth. It turns individual courage into collective intelligence.

Ultimately, the willingness to look stupid is a quiet form of confidence. It signals that you value truth and results over ego. In an industry defined by constant change, the learners will always outpace the knowers.

### Related Tools on ShipMicro
Cultivating this mindset is easier with the right environment. Check out these tools that support fearless experimentation and collaboration:
*   **ShipMicro Pair:** A real-time collaborative editor for frictionless pair programming and instant feedback ([shipmicro.com/tools/pair](https://shipmicro.com/tools/pair)).
*   **SafeBranch:** Isolated, disposable staging environments to test wild ideas without breaking anything ([shipmicro.com/tools/safebranch](https://shipmicro.com/tools/safebranch)).
